title: Pastor Ben Brewer to Churches: Join us for Abolition Month!
author: James Silberman
orientation: A first-person testimony attributed to Pastor Ben Brewer of Blue Bell Free Will Baptist in Sapulpa, Oklahoma, recounting his transition from pro-life to abolitionist and inviting other pastors and churches to participate in Abolition of Abortion Month in January 2021. The piece frames abolitionism as a spiritual call to repentance, preaching, and equal protection for the preborn.
audiences: Pro-life evangelical pastor weighing the abolitionist critique
Lay Christian considering personal involvement in abolition activism
Skeptic of religiously motivated political activism
Historian or researcher tracking the rise of abolitionist pastor networks

questions.question: What heart-level change does Brewer say American Christians need before any policy victory will matter?
questions.audience: Pro-life evangelical pastor weighing the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: Brewer argues the change is not material or political comfort but corporate repentance among pastors, churches, and evangelicals. He frames the underlying problem as slothfulness in the Great Commission rather than mere bad laws, and calls for weeping anguish before God as the necessary precondition for ending abortion.
questions.quote: The change that we pastors, churches, evangelicals, and anyone who associates themselves with Christ need is a heart change. We need to repent.

questions.question: How does Brewer distinguish his earlier pro-life conviction from the abolitionism he now embraces?
questions.audience: Pro-life evangelical pastor weighing the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: He says he had always despised abortion and rejected the excuses offered for it, but other Oklahoma preachers showed him a different doctrine and methodology that reframed abortion as murder requiring immediate abolition rather than incremental regulation, leading him to identify as an abolitionist pastor.
questions.quote: They showed me the difference in abolitionism and pro-life doctrine and methodology and quickly helped me realized I am an abolitionist pastor.

questions.question: On what biblical basis does Brewer indict supporters of abortion, even indirect ones?
questions.audience: Skeptic of religiously motivated political activism
questions.answer: He cites Romans 1:32 to argue that those who support the murder of the unborn directly or indirectly are themselves in sin under God's judgment. The verse, in his framing, extends moral culpability beyond the act itself to anyone who takes pleasure in or endorses it.
questions.quote: I preach that it is a sin to murder the unborn and that anyone who supports it directly or indirectly is in sin also according to Romans 1:32, "Who knowing the judgment of God, that they which commit such things are worthy of death, not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them."

questions.question: Why does Brewer insist abortion is a spiritual matter and not merely a legislative one?
questions.audience: Lay Christian considering personal involvement in abolition activism
questions.answer: He treats abortion law, the rescue of preborn lives, and gospel outreach to mothers as inseparable spiritual concerns. For Brewer, fighting abolition is part of being a soldier of Christ rather than a partisan civic project, which is why he calls pastors to put feet on their faith.
questions.quote: Abortion laws are a spiritual matter, saving the innocent lives that are murdered every day of the year is a spiritual matter, and reaching these mothers with the gospel is a spiritual matter.

questions.question: What pulpit posture does Brewer demand of fellow pastors regarding incremental pro-life regulations?
questions.audience: Pro-life evangelical pastor weighing the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: He calls pastors to wake up, preach hard against sin, offer forgiveness in Christ to women who have had abortions, and explicitly preach against pro-life regulations that he sees as conceding the legitimacy of abortion so long as procedural requirements are met.
questions.quote: Preach hard against these pro-life regulations that that accept the idea that a woman can murder her child as long as they cover every base and jump every hurdle to get to that point.

questions.question: What concrete event is Brewer recruiting churches to join, and when?
questions.answer: He is promoting Abolition of Abortion Month, taking place in January 2021, and asking other pastors to bring their congregations into the fight. The framing combines repentance, anti-apathy preaching, and active participation in the movement.
questions.quote: I hope to see other pastors get involved with their churches in the fight to abolish abortion, to stop the shedding of innocent blood and seek the forgiveness of God for our collective apathy and compromise.

questions.question: How can a pastor or church practically plug into Abolition Month according to the article?
questions.answer: Brewer points readers to the Abolition Month Pastors Group on Facebook, a private group accessible by reaching out to him, Tim Gillespie, Dusty Deevers, other Oklahoma abolitionist pastors, or Free the States. Participating churches receive resources such as educational videos, pamphlets, and weekly themes for engaging their congregations.
questions.quote: You can learn more by joining the [Abolition Month Pastors Group on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/groups/3475031459233397). It's a private group, so reach out to me, Tim Gillespie, Dusty Deevers, other Oklahoma abolitionist pastors, or Free the States and we can add you.

questions.question: What broader vision of saving the preborn does Brewer articulate beyond simply outlawing abortion?
questions.audience: Historian or researcher tracking the rise of abolitionist pastor networks
questions.answer: He frames abolition as evangelistic as well as legal: not just stopping the killing but seeing preborn children grow up to live lives pleasing to God and seeing their parents come to faith in Christ. This positions abolition as part of building the kingdom of God under the Great Commission.
questions.quote: It is about seeing millions of babies saved and given the opportunity to live a life pleasing to God, to see them accept salvation and to see their mothers and fathers come to know Christ in an intimate, personal way.

related.title: It's All About the Church
related.reason: Companion freethestates.org piece arguing the church is the central agent of abolition, directly mirroring Brewer's call for pastoral leadership.

related.title: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
related.reason: Another pastor's testimony in the same series, written one month later, extending the repentance-and-rally framing to Abolition Day.

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: Documents the broader pastor-led push, including Dusty Deevers, that Brewer references when naming Oklahoma abolitionist leadership.

related.title: Free Will Baptist Abolitionist Leaders and Pastors Endorse Warren Hamilton
related.reason: Brewer pastors a Free Will Baptist church; this piece situates his denominational tradition inside the abolitionist coalition he joined.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Earlier Oklahoma pastor-led conflict with denominational leadership that helps explain the abolitionist-vs-pro-life divide Brewer describes.

related.title: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
related.reason: Direct rebuke to congregations that ignore the call Brewer is issuing, useful for readers wrestling with whether to act.

related.title: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
related.reason: Later flashpoint involving Dusty Deevers, one of the Oklahoma pastors Brewer credits, illustrating how the pastor network Brewer joined would be received by establishment evangelicals.
